content.problemStatement:
You are given a string SSS consisting of lowercase english characters. Partition SSS such that every substring of the partition is a palindrome. Find all possible **unique palindrome** partitioning of SSS.

Note:


A substring of a string is obtained by deleting some (possibly zero) characters from the beginning and some (possibly zero) characters from the end.
A palindrome is a string that reads the same backward as forward.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case consists of a string SSS.


Output Format
For each test case, output M+1M+1M+1 lines, where MMM is the number of unique palindrome partitioning:



The first line contains a single integer MMM.
The next MMM lines contains space-separated substring of SSS.

Note:



The partitions must be printed in lexicographically increasing order.

We say that partition XXX is lexicographically smaller than YYY if either XXX is a prefix of YYY or there exists an index iii such that for all j<ij\lt ij<i, Xj=YjX_j = Y_jXj​=Yj​ and Xi<YiX_i\lt Y_iXi​<Yi​.
constraints: S contains only lowercase English letters
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a non‑empty string S consisting of lowercase English letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output M+1 lines, where M is the number of unique palindrome partitions of S. The first line contains M. Each of the next M lines contains one partition: the substrings of S separated by a single space. Partitions must be printed in lexicographically increasing order.

sampleTestCases.output:
2
a a b
aa b
1
a
4
a a a
a aa
aa a
aaa
sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1 (aab): possible palindrome partitions are [a,a,b] and [aa,b]; they are printed in lexicographic order. Test case 2 (a): only [a]. Test case 3 (aaa): partitions are [a,a,a], [a,aa], [aa,a], [aaa] ordered lexicographically.

sampleTestCases.output:
4
a b a b a
a b aba
a bab a
ababa
sampleTestCases.explanation: All palindrome partitions of "ababa" are listed and sorted lexicographically.

sampleTestCases.output:
4
r a c e c a r
r a c ecar
r acecar
racecar
sampleTestCases.explanation: Each substring in a partition must be a palindrome. The four unique partitions are shown in increasing lexicographic order.

solution.approach: For each test case pre‑compute a table isPal[i][j] indicating whether S[i..j] is a palindrome (O(N^2) time). Then run a depth‑first search from index 0. At position pos we try every end index end >= pos such that isPal[pos][end] is true, push the substring S[pos..end] to the current path and recurse from end+1. When pos reaches N we have a complete partition – store a copy of the path. After the search finishes, sort all partitions lexicographically (standard vector<string> comparison works because the vectors are compared element by element). Finally output the count and the sorted partitions. The algorithm explores each possible cut pattern once, so the time complexity is O(N·2^N) in the worst case (N ≤ 15) and the extra space is O(N^2) for the palindrome table plus O(N) recursion depth.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2 + N·2^N) per test case (palindrome table + backtracking). With N ≤ 15 this easily fits the limits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N^2) for the palindrome table plus O(N) for the recursion stack and current partition.
